instruction:
The general solution of the equation $y' \sec^2 y - \sec^2 y - 1 = 0$ is ( ).

Options:
A. $$\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \arctan \frac{\tan y}{\sqrt{3}}=x+C, (C \text{ is an arbitrary constant})$$
B. $$\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \arctan \frac{\sin y}{\sqrt{2}}=x+C, (C \text{ is an arbitrary constant})$$
C. $$\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \arctan \frac{\tan x}{\sqrt{2}}=x+C, (C \text{ is an arbitrary constant})$$
D. $$\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \arctan \frac{\cot y}{\sqrt{2}}=x+C, (C \text{ is an arbitrary constant})$$
E. $$\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \arctan \frac{\cos y}{\sqrt{2}}=x+C, (C \text{ is an arbitrary constant})$$
F. $$\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \arctan \frac{\tan x}{\sqrt{2}}=y+C, (C \text{ is an arbitrary constant})$$
G. $$\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \arctan \frac{\csc y}{\sqrt{2}}=x+C, (C \text{ is an arbitrary constant})$$
H. $$\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \arctan \frac{\sec y}{\sqrt{2}}=x+C, (C \text{ is an arbitrary constant})$$
I. $$\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \arctan \frac{\tan y}{\sqrt{2}}=x+C, (C \text{ is an arbitrary constant})$$
